- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- gsd-advisor-researcher: Researches a single gray area decision and returns a structured comparison table with rationale. Spawned by discuss-phase advisor mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-ai-researcher: Researches a chosen AI framework's official docs to produce implementation-ready guidance — best practices, syntax, core patterns, and pitfalls distilled for the specific use case. Writes the Framework Quick Reference and Implementation Guidance sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)
- gsd-assumptions-analyzer: Deeply analyzes codebase for a phase and returns structured assumptions with evidence. Spawned by discuss-phase assumptions mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-fixer: Applies fixes to code review findings from REVIEW.md. Reads source files, applies intelligent fixes, and commits each fix atomically. Spawned by /gsd-code-review --fix. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-reviewer: Reviews source files for bugs, security issues, and code quality problems. Produces structured REVIEW.md with severity-classified findings. Spawned by /gsd-code-review. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-codebase-mapper: Explores codebase and writes structured analysis documents. Spawned by map-codebase with a focus area (tech, arch, quality, concerns). Writes documents directly to reduce orchestrator context load. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Skill)

- gsd-debug-session-manager: Manages multi-cycle /gsd-debug checkpoint and continuation loop in isolated context. Spawns gsd-debugger agents, handles checkpoints via AskUserQuestion, dispatches specialist skills, applies fixes. Returns compact summary to main context. Spawned by /gsd-debug command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Agent, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-debugger: Investigates bugs using scientific method, manages debug sessions, handles checkpoints. Spawned by /gsd-debug orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch)

- gsd-doc-classifier: Classifies a single planning document as ADR, PRD, SPEC, DOC, or UNKNOWN. Extracts title, scope summary, and cross-references. Spawned in parallel by /gsd-ingest-docs. Writes a JSON classification file and returns a one-line confirmation. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-synthesizer: Synthesizes classified planning docs into a single consolidated context. Applies precedence rules, detects cross-ref cycles, enforces LOCKED-vs-LOCKED hard-blocks, and writes INGEST-CONFLICTS.md with three buckets (auto-resolved, competing-variants, unresolved-blockers). Spawned by /gsd-ingest-docs. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- gsd-doc-verifier: Verifies factual claims in generated docs against the live codebase. Returns structured JSON per doc. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-writer: Writes and updates project documentation. Spawned with a doc_assignment block specifying doc type, mode (create/update/supplement), and project context. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Skill)

- gsd-domain-researcher: Researches the business domain and real-world application context of the AI system being built. Surfaces domain expert evaluation criteria, industry-specific failure modes, regulatory context, and what "good" looks like for practitioners in this field — before the eval-planner turns it into measurable rubrics. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-eval-auditor: Retroactive audit of an implemented AI phase's evaluation coverage. Checks implementation against the AI-SPEC.md evaluation plan. Scores each eval dimension as COVERED/PARTIAL/MISSING. Produces a scored EVAL-REVIEW.md with findings, gaps, and remediation guidance. Spawned by /gsd-eval-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-eval-planner: Designs a structured evaluation strategy for an AI phase. Identifies critical failure modes, selects eval dimensions with rubrics, recommends tooling, and specifies the reference dataset. Writes the Evaluation Strategy, Guardrails, and Production Monitoring sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-executor: Executes GSD plans with atomic commits, deviation handling, checkpoint protocols, and state management. Spawned by execute-phase orchestrator or execute-plan command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-framework-selector: Presents an interactive decision matrix to surface the right AI/LLM framework for the user's specific use case. Produces a scored recommendation with rationale. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase and /gsd-select-framework orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, AskUserQuestion)
- gsd-integration-checker: Verifies cross-phase integration and E2E flows. Checks that phases connect properly and user workflows complete end-to-end. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-intel-updater: Analyzes codebase and writes structured intel files to .planning/intel/. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- gsd-mempalace-curator: Ship-time MemPalace curation — writes the session diary, proposes/creates cross-project tunnels, mirrors extract-learnings into the temporal KG, and runs wing-scoped drawer pruning. Spawned at ship:post by the mempalace capability. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob)
- gsd-nyquist-auditor: Fills Nyquist validation gaps by generating tests and verifying coverage for phase requirements (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-pattern-mapper: Analyzes codebase for existing patterns and produces PATTERNS.md mapping new files to closest analogs. Read-only codebase analysis spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator before planning. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Write)

- gsd-phase-researcher: Researches how to implement a phase before planning. Produces RESEARCH.md consumed by gsd-planner. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)
- gsd-plan-checker: Verifies plans will achieve phase goal before execution. Goal-backward analysis of plan quality. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-planner: Creates executable phase plans with task breakdown, dependency analysis, and goal-backward verification. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-project-researcher: Researches domain ecosystem before roadmap creation. Produces files in .planning/research/ consumed during roadmap creation. Spawned by /gsd-new-project or /gsd-new-milestone orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)

- gsd-research-synthesizer: Synthesizes research outputs from parallel researcher agents into SUMMARY.md. Spawned by /gsd-new-project after 4 researcher agents complete. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Skill)

- gsd-roadmapper: Creates project roadmaps with phase breakdown, requirement mapping, success criteria derivation, and coverage validation. Spawned by /gsd-new-project orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-security-auditor: Verifies threat mitigations from PLAN.md threat model exist in implemented code. Returns structured security verdict (SECURED / OPEN_THREATS / ESCALATE). Spawned by /gsd-secure-phase. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)
- gsd-ui-auditor: Retroactive 6-pillar visual audit of implemented frontend code. Produces scored UI-REVIEW.md. Spawned by /gsd-ui-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-ui-checker: Validates UI-SPEC.md design contracts against 6 quality dimensions. Produces BLOCK/FLAG/PASS verdicts. Spawned by /gsd-ui-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-ui-researcher: Produces UI-SPEC.md design contract for frontend phases. Reads upstream artifacts, detects design system state, asks only unanswered questions. Spawned by /gsd-ui-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*)

- gsd-user-profiler: Analyzes extracted session messages across 8 behavioral dimensions to produce a scored developer profile with confidence levels and evidence. Spawned by profile orchestration workflows. (Tools: Read)

- gsd-verifier: Verifies phase goal achievement through goal-backward analysis. Checks codebase delivers what phase promised, not just that tasks completed. Creates VERIFICATION.md report. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.

- gsd-ai-integration-phase: Generate an AI-SPEC.md design contract for phases that involve building AI systems.

- gsd-autonomous: Run all remaining phases autonomously — discuss→plan→execute per phase
- gsd-capture: Capture ideas, tasks, notes, and seeds to their destination
- gsd-cleanup: Archive accumulated phase directories from completed milestones

- gsd-complete-milestone: Archive completed milestone and prepare for next version

- gsd-extract-learnings: Extract decisions, lessons, patterns, and surprises from completed phase artifacts
- gsd-fast: Execute a trivial task inline — no subagents, no planning overhead
- gsd-forensics: Post-mortem investigation for failed GSD workflows — diagnoses what went wrong.
- gsd-graphify: Build, query, and inspect the project knowledge graph in .planning/graphs/

- gsd-plan-review-convergence: Cross-AI plan convergence - replan until review concerns are resolved.

- gsd-undo: Safe git revert. Roll back phase or plan commits using the phase manifest with dependency checks.

- gsd-workstreams: Manage parallel workstreams — list, create, switch, status, progress, complete, and resume
- merge-main: Merge the base branch (PR target, else main) into the current branch, resolving conflicts locally including a smart 3-way merge of GSD .planning/ files, then verify, push, and wait for CI.
- wait-for-ci: Block until a GitHub PR's CI checks reach a terminal state after a push, then report the final result. Use whenever you need to watch/wait on PR checks (gh pr checks), poll a pipeline after pushing, or babysit CI to completion — in any project.
- do-issue: Autonomous issue-to-PR workflow using GSD. Argument: GitHub issue number.
- review-code-structure: Review the python code of this project with a focus on code architecture and code design. Elimina...
- speckit.analyze: Perform a non-destructive cross-artifact consistency and quality analysis across spec.md, plan.md, and tasks.md after task generation.
- speckit.checklist: Generate a custom checklist for the current feature based on user requirements.
- speckit.clarify: Identify underspecified areas in the current feature spec by asking up to 5 highly targeted clarification questions and encoding answers back into the spec.
- speckit.consolidate: Consolidate SpecKit artifacts into the Golden Copy living docs in docs/system/ per ADR-011
- speckit.constitution: Create or update the project constitution from interactive or provided principle inputs, ensuring all dependent templates stay in sync.
- speckit.implement: Execute the implementation plan by processing and executing all tasks defined in tasks.md

- speckit.plan: Execute the implementation planning workflow using the plan template to generate design artifacts.
- speckit.plan.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback on a speckit feature plan
- speckit.plan.resolve-follow-up: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature implementation
- speckit.plan.review-architecture: Review a speckit feature plan
- speckit.plan.review-detail: Review a speckit feature plan
- speckit.specify: Create or update the feature specification from a natural language feature description.
- speckit.specify.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature plan
- speckit.specify.review: Review a speckit feature specification

- speckit.tasks.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature tasks list
- speckit.taskstoissues: Convert existing tasks into actionable, dependency-ordered GitHub issues for the feature based on available design artifacts.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: update the config.yaml on my system to the new way of configuring filters for folder sync

aiTitle: Update config.yaml for folder sync filters

content.text: I'll first understand the new filter configuration format and find your config file.

input.command: git log --oneline -5 fb5c91f; echo "---"; git show fb5c91f --stat | head -40
input.description: Show the filter-change commit

content.content:

b04f3f1 docs(folder_sync): document filter_file as optional and clarify pattern-match semantics (#166)
0108031 docs(folder_sync): trim the filter-rules section to behavior + syntax only (#166)
b11231e docs(folder_sync): refresh README + configuration.md for the filter_file surface (#166)
5e03d36 docs: trim rederivable content from CLAUDE.md
---
commit fb5c91f73050d4ae3c55bbec8659f0ecc47d2542
Merge: 5e03d36 b04f3f1
Author: Jan Frederik Léger <18148910+janfrederik@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   Sun Jul 19 00:28:26 2026 +0200

    folder_sync: include-override filter rules via native rsync filter syntax (#166) (#173)
    
    Closes #117
    Fixes #166
    
    ## Summary
    
    Replaces `folder_sync`'s exclude-only config surface with **native rsync
    filter syntax** (`+`/`-`, first-match-wins), so a user can express
    "exclude a folder but keep select subfolders" — e.g. drop most of
    `~/.cache` but keep `~/.cache/uv` and `~/.cache/pip`. Two authoring
    surfaces:
    
    - **Central filter file (per folder)** — a new optional `filter_file`
    field on each folder entry (replaces `excludes`), fed to rsync as
    `--filter='merge <file>'`.
    - **Per-directory `.pcswitcher-filter`** — a single tree-wide
    `--filter='dir-merge /.pcswitcher-filter'` rule; behaves like
    `.gitignore` (inherits into subdirs) and the files themselves sync to
    the target (no `e` modifier).
    
    **Precedence: GLOBAL-FIRST.** `_build_rsync_cmd` emits filter args in
    order: runtime-protection excludes (ADR-016, un-overridable) → central
    `merge` (when set) → `dir-merge` (always). A per-dir `+` can never
    re-expose a globally-excluded path on a `--delete` mirror.
    
    Other changes:
    - `pc-switcher init` now ships starter `home.filter` + `root.filter`
    (package data) next to `config.yaml`, honoring `--force`.
    - `validate()` fails fast with a source-side `ValidationError` when a
    configured `filter_file` is absent (after `~`/env expansion), guarding
    against a silent full-mirror.
    - Never passes `-F`/`-FF`/`-C`/`--cvs-exclude`, so a stray/hostile
    `.rsync-filter` in the tree has zero effect.
    - ADR-016 and `docs/configuration.md` updated: the new filter surface,

1	# PC-switcher configuration file
2	# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
3	# Documentation: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher
4	# Full config reference: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/blob/main/docs/configuration.md
5	
6	# =============================================================================
7	# Logging Configuration (3-setting model)
8	# =============================================================================
9	# Controls where log messages appear based on their severity level.
10	#
11	# Log levels (from most to least verbose):
12	#   DEBUG (10)    - Internal diagnostics, very verbose
13	#   FULL (15)     - Operational details (file-level sync info)
14	#   INFO (20)     - High-level progress and status updates
15	#   WARNING (30)  - Unexpected but non-fatal conditions
16	#   ERROR (40)    - Recoverable errors
17	#   CRITICAL (50) - Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort
18	
19	logging:
20	  # file: Floor level for log FILE output (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
21	  #       Messages at or above this level are written to the log file.
22	  file: DEBUG
23	
24	  # tui: Floor level for TERMINAL output during sync
25	  #      Messages at or above this level are shown in the terminal UI.
26	  tui: INFO
27	
28	  # external: Additional floor for THIRD-PARTY libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
29	  #           External library logs must pass BOTH this floor AND the
30	  #           destination floor (file or tui) to appear in output.
31	  #           Use DEBUG to see SSH connection details, WARNING to suppress noise.
32	  external: WARNING
33	
34	# =============================================================================
35	# Sync Jobs Configuration
36	# =============================================================================
37	# Enable or disable optional sync jobs
38	# Note: Only jobs explicitly listed here will be discovered and run
39	# Set to false to disable a job (e.g., dummy_fail: false)
40	
41	sync_jobs:
42	  # Test jobs (for development/testing)
43	  dummy_success: false   # Test job that completes successfully
44	  dummy_fail: false      # Test job that fails at configurable progress
45	
46	  folder_sync: true          # Sync /home and /root directories (Phase 1)
47	
48	  # Future jobs (uncomment when available):
49	  # packages: true        # Sync apt, snap, flatpak packages
50	  # docker: false         # Sync Docker images, containers, volumes
51	  # vms: false            # Sync KVM/virt-manager VMs
52	  # k3s: false            # Sync k3s cluster state
53	
54	# =============================================================================
55	# Disk Space Monitoring
56	# =============================================================================
57	# Configuration for disk space checks before and during sync
58	# Threshold format: percentage (e.g., "20%") or absolute (e.g., "50GiB")
59	
60	disk_space_monitor:
61	  # Minimum free space required before sync starts
62	  preflight_minimum: "20%"
63	
64	  # Minimum free space required during sync (CRITICAL - abort if below)
65	  runtime_minimum: "15%"
66	
67	  # Free space threshold for warnings during sync
68	  warning_threshold: "25%"
69	
70	  # Seconds between disk space checks during sync
71	  check_interval: 30
72	
73	# =============================================================================
74	# Btrfs Snapshot Management
75	# =============================================================================
76	# Configuration for automatic btrfs snapshots before and after sync
77	# IMPORTANT: Subvolumes must match YOUR system's btrfs layout
78	
79	btrfs_snapshots:
80	  # List of btrfs subvolumes to snapshot
81	  # Check your system: sudo btrfs subvolume list /
82	  # Common layouts:
83	  #   - Ubuntu default: ["@", "@home"]
84	  #   - Single root: ["@"]
85	  subvolumes:
86	    - "@"
87	    - "@home"
88	
89	  # Number of recent sync sessions to always retain snapshots for,
90	  # irrespective of age (max_age_days below)
91	  keep_recent: 3
92	
93	  # Maximum age in days for snapshot retention (optional)
94	  # Note that always keeping 'keep_recent' snapshots takes precedence.
95	  # Uncomment to enable age-based cleanup:
96	  # max_age_days: 7
97	
98	# =============================================================================
99	# Job-Specific Configuration
100	# =============================================================================
101	# Each sync job can have its own configuration section
102	# Configuration keys must match the job name
103	
104	# Test job configurations (examples)
105	dummy_success:
106	  source_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on source machine
107	  target_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on target machine
108	
109	dummy_fail:
110	  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
111	  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
112	  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail
113	
114	# =============================================================================
115	# Folder Sync Configuration
116	# =============================================================================
117	# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry is a full mirror (rsync
118	# --delete): everything under `path` is synced EXCEPT the paths matched by
119	# that folder's exclude list. There is no include mechanism — to narrow what
120	# is synced, use a deeper `path` or add more excludes.
121	#
122	# Each exclude is an rsync filter pattern, relative to the folder's transfer
123	# root (for /home the root is its contents, so `.ssh/id_*` matches every
124	# user's .ssh). Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom, first match wins. Syntax:
125	#   - No leading / floats: matches at any depth (`.cache/nvidia` matches
126	#     `.cache/nvidia` and `alice/.cache/nvidia`).
127	#   - Leading / anchors to the folder root (`/lost+found`).
128	#   - A pattern with no / matches the final path component only (`nvidia`);
129	#     one containing a / or ** matches the full path.
130	#   - Trailing / restricts to directories (`node_modules/`).
131	#   - `*` matches within a segment, `**` matches across /.
132	# Full syntax (rsync "FILTER RULES"): https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1
133	#
134	# Excluded below: machine-specific keys/config, GPU/shader caches, VS Code
135	# regenerable caches. Users can override by editing this file.
136	#
137	# Always excluded automatically (not listed here, cannot be overridden): pc-switcher's
138	# own runtime files — ~/.local/share/pc-switcher/ (state, lock, logs), the uv-tool
139	# install, and the ~/.local/bin/pc-switcher shim (ADR-016).
140	
141	folder_sync:
142	  folders:
143	    - path: /home/janfr
144	      enabled: true
145	      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
146	      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
147	      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
148	      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
149	      excludes:
150	
151	        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
152	        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
153	        - /.ssh/id_*
154	        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
155	        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
156	        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
157	        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.
158	
159	        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
160	        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
161	        - /.local/share/keyrings
162	
163	        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
164	        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
165	        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.
166	
167	        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
168	        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
169	        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
170	        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
171	        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
172	        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
173	        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
174	        - /.config/monitors.xml~
175	        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays
176	
177	        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
178	        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
179	        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
180	        - /.cache/thumbnails
181	        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
182	        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches
183	
184	        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
185	        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
186	        - /.cache/google-chrome
187	        - /.cache/spotify
188	        - /.cache/electron
189	        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
190	        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
191	        - /.cache/plasmashell
192	        - /.config/Code/Cache
193	        - /.config/Code/CachedData
194	        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
195	        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
196	        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
197	        - /.config/Claude/Cache
198	        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
199	        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
200	        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
201	        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
202	        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)
203	
204	        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
205	        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
206	        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
207	        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
208	        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
209	        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
210	        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
211	        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
212	        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
213	        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
214	        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can
215	
216	        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
217	        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
218	        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
219	        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
220	        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
221	        - /.local/share/flatpak
222	
223	        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
224	        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link
225	
226	        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
227	        # Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
228	        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
229	        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
230	        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
231	        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
232	        # links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
233	        # to those daemons entirely.
234	        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
235	        # Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
236	        # (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
237	        # leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
238	        # syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
239	        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
240	        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
241	        - /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
242	        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon
243	
244	        # --- Session runtime junk ---
245	        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot
246

file.content:
# PC-switcher configuration file
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
# Documentation: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher
# Full config reference: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/blob/main/docs/configuration.md

# =============================================================================
# Logging Configuration (3-setting model)
# =============================================================================
# Controls where log messages appear based on their severity level.
#
# Log levels (from most to least verbose):
#   DEBUG (10)    - Internal diagnostics, very verbose
#   FULL (15)     - Operational details (file-level sync info)
#   INFO (20)     - High-level progress and status updates
#   WARNING (30)  - Unexpected but non-fatal conditions
#   ERROR (40)    - Recoverable errors
#   CRITICAL (50) - Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort

logging:
  # file: Floor level for log FILE output (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  #       Messages at or above this level are written to the log file.
  file: DEBUG

  # tui: Floor level for TERMINAL output during sync
  #      Messages at or above this level are shown in the terminal UI.
  tui: INFO

  # external: Additional floor for THIRD-PARTY libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
  #           External library logs must pass BOTH this floor AND the
  #           destination floor (file or tui) to appear in output.
  #           Use DEBUG to see SSH connection details, WARNING to suppress noise.
  external: WARNING

# =============================================================================
# Sync Jobs Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Enable or disable optional sync jobs
# Note: Only jobs explicitly listed here will be discovered and run
# Set to false to disable a job (e.g., dummy_fail: false)

sync_jobs:
  # Test jobs (for development/testing)
  dummy_success: false   # Test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false      # Test job that fails at configurable progress

  folder_sync: true          # Sync /home and /root directories (Phase 1)

  # Future jobs (uncomment when available):
  # packages: true        # Sync apt, snap, flatpak packages
  # docker: false         # Sync Docker images, containers, volumes
  # vms: false            # Sync KVM/virt-manager VMs
  # k3s: false            # Sync k3s cluster state

# =============================================================================
# Disk Space Monitoring
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for disk space checks before and during sync
# Threshold format: percentage (e.g., "20%") or absolute (e.g., "50GiB")

disk_space_monitor:
  # Minimum free space required before sync starts
  preflight_minimum: "20%"

  # Minimum free space required during sync (CRITICAL - abort if below)
  runtime_minimum: "15%"

  # Free space threshold for warnings during sync
  warning_threshold: "25%"

  # Seconds between disk space checks during sync
  check_interval: 30

# =============================================================================
# Btrfs Snapshot Management
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for automatic btrfs snapshots before and after sync
# IMPORTANT: Subvolumes must match YOUR system's btrfs layout

btrfs_snapshots:
  # List of btrfs subvolumes to snapshot
  # Check your system: sudo btrfs subvolume list /
  # Common layouts:
  #   - Ubuntu default: ["@", "@home"]
  #   - Single root: ["@"]
  subvolumes:
    - "@"
    - "@home"

  # Number of recent sync sessions to always retain snapshots for,
  # irrespective of age (max_age_days below)
  keep_recent: 3

  # Maximum age in days for snapshot retention (optional)
  # Note that always keeping 'keep_recent' snapshots takes precedence.
  # Uncomment to enable age-based cleanup:
  # max_age_days: 7

# =============================================================================
# Job-Specific Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Each sync job can have its own configuration section
# Configuration keys must match the job name

# Test job configurations (examples)
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on target machine

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail

# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry is a full mirror (rsync
# --delete): everything under `path` is synced EXCEPT the paths matched by
# that folder's exclude list. There is no include mechanism — to narrow what
# is synced, use a deeper `path` or add more excludes.
#
# Each exclude is an rsync filter pattern, relative to the folder's transfer
# root (for /home the root is its contents, so `.ssh/id_*` matches every
# user's .ssh). Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom, first match wins. Syntax:
#   - No leading / floats: matches at any depth (`.cache/nvidia` matches
#     `.cache/nvidia` and `alice/.cache/nvidia`).
#   - Leading / anchors to the folder root (`/lost+found`).
#   - A pattern with no / matches the final path component only (`nvidia`);
#     one containing a / or ** matches the full path.
#   - Trailing / restricts to directories (`node_modules/`).
#   - `*` matches within a segment, `**` matches across /.
# Full syntax (rsync "FILTER RULES"): https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1
#
# Excluded below: machine-specific keys/config, GPU/shader caches, VS Code
# regenerable caches. Users can override by editing this file.
#
# Always excluded automatically (not listed here, cannot be overridden): pc-switcher's
# own runtime files — ~/.local/share/pc-switcher/ (state, lock, logs), the uv-tool
# install, and the ~/.local/bin/pc-switcher shim (ADR-016).

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
        # to those daemons entirely.
        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
        # Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
        # (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
        # leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
        # syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

input.description: Read configuration docs

content.content:
# Configuration Reference

Complete reference for `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`. Run `pc-switcher init` to write the annotated default file (`src/pcswitcher/default-config.yaml`), then edit it.

The config is validated against a JSON Schema on load (`src/pcswitcher/schemas/config-schema.yaml`). Unknown top-level keys and unknown job names are rejected. Both source and target run with the same config: it is copied to the target during sync (step 10 of the sync sequence).

## `logging`

Controls where log messages appear, based on severity. Three independent floors — a message is emitted to a destination when its level is at or above that destination's floor.

```yaml
logging:
  file: DEBUG        # floor for the log file (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  tui: INFO          # floor for terminal output during sync
  external: WARNING  # additional floor for third-party libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
```

### Log levels

| Level | Value | Description |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| DEBUG | 10 | Internal diagnostics, very verbose |
| FULL | 15 | Operational details (file-level sync info) |
| INFO | 20 | High-level progress (job start/complete) |
| WARNING | 30 | Unexpected but non-fatal conditions |
| ERROR | 40 | Recoverable errors |
| CRITICAL | 50 | Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort |

`file` and `tui` set the floor for their destination. `external` is an *additional* floor applied only to third-party library logs: an external log must clear **both** `external` and the destination floor (`file` or `tui`) to appear. Use `external: DEBUG` to surface asyncssh connection details; keep it at `WARNING` to suppress library noise.

Log files are written in JSON-lines format to `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/`.

Debug SSH connection issues:

```yaml
logging:
  file: DEBUG
  tui: INFO
  external: DEBUG  # show asyncssh debug logs
```

Quiet terminal, full log file:

```yaml
logging:
  file: DEBUG   # still record everything to the file
  tui: ERROR    # show only errors in the terminal
  external: ERROR
```

## `sync_jobs`

Enables or disables optional sync jobs. Only job names listed here are discovered and run; unknown names fail validation.

```yaml
sync_jobs:
  dummy_success: true   # test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false     # test job that fails at a configurable time
  folder_sync: true     # sync /home and /root via rsync
```

Future jobs (`packages`, `docker`, `vms`, `k3s`) are added here as they are implemented.

## `disk_space_monitor`

Disk-space checks before and during a sync. Thresholds are either a percentage (`"20%"`) or an absolute size (`"50GiB"`, `"500MiB"`, `"50GB"`, `"500MB"`).

```yaml
disk_space_monitor:
  preflight_minimum: "20%"   # required free space before sync starts
  runtime_minimum: "15%"     # required free space during sync — CRITICAL, aborts if crossed
  warning_threshold: "25%"   # logs a WARNING while free space is below this
  check_interval: 30         # seconds between checks during sync (5–300)
```

`preflight_minimum` is checked on both hosts before any mutation; a shortfall aborts the sync. `runtime_minimum` is enforced by a background monitor while jobs run and aborts the sync if crossed. `warning_threshold` only logs.

## `btrfs_snapshots`

Automatic btrfs snapshots taken before and after a sync (the rollback point). Subvolumes **must** match your machine's layout — check with `sudo btrfs subvolume list /`.

```yaml
btrfs_snapshots:
  subvolumes:        # subvolume names, each must start with @
    - "@"
    - "@home"
  keep_recent: 3     # always retain snapshots for the N most recent sync sessions
  # max_age_days: 7  # optional: also delete snapshots older than N days
```

`keep_recent` takes precedence over `max_age_days`: the N most recent sessions are always kept regardless of age. `max_age_days` is optional; omit it to disable age-based cleanup.

## `dummy_success` / `dummy_fail`

Per-job config for the test jobs (development only).

```yaml
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20   # seconds to run on source
  target_duration: 20   # seconds to run on target

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10
  target_duration: 10
  fail_at: 12           # elapsed seconds at which to fail
```

## `folder_sync`

Folders to sync via rsync-over-SSH, running as root on both ends to preserve ownership, permissions, ACLs, and xattrs.

```yaml
folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter
    - path: /root
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/root.filter
```

Each folder is mirrored to the target, minus the paths its filter rules exclude. Set `enabled: false` to skip a folder. A filtered-out file is left untouched on the target if it already exists there, so machine-specific files (SSH keys, Tailscale config) can stay independent on each machine.

### Filter rules

Filter rules decide what is and isn't synced. They live in two kinds of file:

- **`filter_file`** — the folder's main rule list. Optional: omit it for a folder that needs no central rule list, and only the runtime excludes and any `.pcswitcher-filter` files apply. `pc-switcher init` writes `home.filter` and `root.filter` next to `config.yaml`; edit them to taste. If a folder *does* configure a `filter_file` but the file is missing, the sync stops with an error.
- **`.pcswitcher-filter`** — a per-directory rule file that works like `.gitignore`: drop one into any directory and its rules apply to that directory and everything below. These files sync to the target too.

Each rule is `- pattern` to exclude or `+ pattern` to re-include. The **first** matching rule wins, so put a `+` re-include before the broader `-` it carves out of. If a path is matched in both a `filter_file` and a `.pcswitcher-filter`, the `filter_file` wins. This allows for central policy that can not be overridden by a per-directory file.

Pattern syntax:

- names match in full, never as substrings: each wildcard-free component must match a whole file or directory name — `foo` matches an entry named exactly `foo`, not `fool` or `afoo`. Use wildcards for partial names (`foo*`, `*foo*`).
- a pattern with no `/` is matched against the final path component (a single file or directory name); a pattern with a `/` is matched against the whole path below the folder root
- a leading `/` anchors to the folder root; without it a pattern matches at any depth
- a trailing `/` matches directories only; without it a pattern matches either a file or a directory of that name
- `?` matches one character, `*` matches within a path segment, `**` matches across `/`, and `[…]` is a character class
- `dir/**` matches a directory's contents but **not** the directory entry itself; `dir/***` matches the directory *and* everything in it. Reach for `/***` when a later exclude (like `- .cache/*`) would otherwise drop the directory and stop rsync from descending into it

| Pattern | Matches |
|---------|---------|
| `.ssh/id_*` | `id_*` inside any `.ssh` directory |
| `.config/tailscale` | a `tailscale` file or directory inside any `.config` |
| `/lost+found` | `lost+found` only at the folder root |
| `*.tmp` | any `.tmp` file, at any depth |
| `node_modules/` | any directory named `node_modules` |

Full pattern reference: rsync's manpage, "FILTER RULES" section — https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1

### Keep a subfolder inside an excluded one

To drop most of a directory but keep selected children — for example drop `~/.cache` but keep the dev-tool caches so offline work doesn't re-download them — re-include the children before excluding the rest:

```
+ .cache/
+ .cache/uv/***
+ .cache/pip/***
- .cache/*
```

The `/***` (not `/**`) matters here: it re-includes the `uv` and `pip` directory entries themselves, so the following `- .cache/*` can't drop them and rsync descends to copy their contents. The shipped `home.filter` already does this; add more `+ .cache/<tool>/***` lines for other caches you want to keep.

### Coming from .gitignore

The syntax resembles `.gitignore`, with three differences worth knowing:

- **Signs, not `!`** — `- pattern` excludes, `+ pattern` re-includes.
- **First match wins** (the opposite of gitignore) — order rules from specific to general.
- **Only a leading `/` anchors** — a middle slash does not, so for `/home` keep patterns unanchored to match under every user's home.

Otherwise it matches gitignore (basenames, a trailing `/` for directories, `*`/`**`/`[…]`), and a `.pcswitcher-filter` behaves like a committed `.gitignore`.

### Always excluded

pc-switcher's own files are always excluded and cannot be re-included, so a sync never disturbs the target's sync state or the running install: `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/`, `~/.local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher`, and `~/.local/bin/pc-switcher`.

input.command: cd /home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher; find . -name "*.filter" -not -path "./.git/*"; echo "--- default config ---"; sed -n '110,200p' src/pcswitcher/default-config.yaml 2>/dev/null
input.description: Find shipped filter files and default config folder_sync section

content.content:
./src/pcswitcher/home.filter
./src/pcswitcher/root.filter
--- default config ---
  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail

# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry mirrors `path` (rsync --delete)
# minus the paths matched by its filter rules. Two authoring surfaces are
# available, both native rsync filter syntax (`+` include, `-` exclude,
# first-match-wins):
#   - filter_file: a per-folder central filter, spliced via rsync `merge`.
#     Optional; omit it for a folder that needs no central rule list (the
#     runtime excludes and any .pcswitcher-filter files still apply).
#   - .pcswitcher-filter: a tree-wide per-directory file, spliced via rsync
#     `dir-merge`; it inherits into subdirectories and can re-include a
#     subtree a broader exclude would otherwise drop (e.g. keep ~/.cache/uv
#     while dropping the rest of ~/.cache).
# GLOBAL-FIRST precedence: the central filter_file is evaluated before any
# .pcswitcher-filter, so central policy always wins. pc-switcher's own runtime
# files (ADR-016) are excluded first of all and can never be re-exposed by
# either surface.
#
# See docs/configuration.md for the full filter-rule syntax and a guide for
# users coming from .gitignore.

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter

    - path: /root
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/root.filter

# Future job configurations will be added here when features are implemented:

file.content:
# pc-switcher central filter file for the /home folder_sync entry.
# Native rsync filter syntax: `+` includes, `-` excludes, first-match-wins.
# Patterns here are FLOATING (no leading /): pc-switcher syncs /home with each
# user's directory as the transfer root's immediate content (e.g. alice/.cache/uv),
# so a leading-slash pattern would anchor to /home itself and never match.
# See docs/configuration.md "Filter rules" and "Coming from .gitignore" for the
# full syntax and the anchored-vs-floating distinction.

# Machine-identity excludes — never synced between machines.
- .ssh/id_*
- .ssh/known_hosts
# authorized_keys is machine-local access control: mirroring it overwrites the
# target's copy (which does not list the source), so the target rejects the
# source's next connection and locks the sync out of its own target.
- .ssh/authorized_keys
- .config/tailscale

# Folder sync job configuration (FolderSyncJob)
  # NOTE: Config key matches job name ("folder_sync") per naming convention below
  folder_sync:
    type: object
    description: "Configuration for FolderSyncJob (generic folder sync via rsync)"
    properties:
      folders:
        type: array
        items:
          type: object
          properties:
            path:
              type: string
              description: "Absolute path to sync (e.g., /home)"
            enabled:
              type: boolean
              default: true
              description: "Whether to sync this folder (default true)"
            filter_file:
              type: string
              description: "Path to a central rsync merge filter file for this folder (~ and env vars expanded); unset means no central filter rule"
          required: [path]
          additionalProperties: false
        minItems: 1
        description: "List of folder entries to sync"
    required: [folders]
    additionalProperties: false

# =============================================================================

content.text:
Confirmed: the old `excludes:` field is no longer valid — the folder schema now only allows `path`, `enabled`, `filter_file` (with `additionalProperties: false`), so your current config would fail validation. Migration is necessary.

Here's my plan:
- Create `~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter` with all your excludes ported to native rsync `-`/`+` syntax, keeping the leading `/` anchors (valid because your `path` is `/home/janfr`) and preserving your rationale comments.
- Rewrite the `folder_sync` section of `config.yaml` to use `filter_file:` instead of `excludes:`.

One deviation I'll flag rather than do silently: your current excludes don't list `.ssh/authorized_keys`. Mirroring it overwrites the target's copy and locks the sync out of its own target (a documented lockout, now in the shipped default). I'll add it with a comment — remove it if you disagree. I'm preserving your deliberate choice to keep `known_hosts` synced.

Let me write the filter file:

input.content:
# pc-switcher central filter file for the /home/janfr folder_sync entry.
# Native rsync filter syntax: `+` includes, `-` excludes, first-match-wins.
# The transfer root is /home/janfr's contents, so a leading `/` anchors to the
# top level of this home dir (e.g. `- /.ssh/id_*` == /home/janfr/.ssh/id_*).
# See docs/configuration.md "Filter rules" and "Coming from .gitignore".

# --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
# SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
- /.ssh/id_*
# authorized_keys is machine-local access control: mirroring it overwrites the
# target's copy (which does not list the source), so the target rejects the
# source's next connection and locks the sync out of its own target.
- /.ssh/authorized_keys
# NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
# you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
# laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
# other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

# GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
# would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
- /.local/share/keyrings

# NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
# store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
# pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

# --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
- /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
- /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
- /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
- /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
- /.config/monitors.xml~
- /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

# --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
- /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
- /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
- /.cache/thumbnails
- /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
- /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

# --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
# (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
- /.cache/google-chrome
- /.cache/spotify
- /.cache/electron
- /.cache/IBKR Desktop
- /.cache/falco-app-updater
- /.cache/plasmashell
- /.config/Code/Cache
- /.config/Code/CachedData
- /.config/Code/GPUCache
- /.config/Code/Code Cache
- /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
- /.config/Claude/Cache
- /.config/Claude/Code Cache
- /.config/Claude/GPUCache
- /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
- /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
- /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
# Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
# names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
# from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
# local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
# so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
# fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
# Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
# (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
- /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
- /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

# --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
# Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
# content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
# IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
# are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
- /.local/share/flatpak

# --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
- /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

# --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
# Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
# Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
# is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
# conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
# daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
# links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
# to those daemons entirely.
# NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
# Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
# (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
# leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
# syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
- /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
- /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
- /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
- /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

# --- Session runtime junk ---
- /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

content.text: Now rewrite the `folder_sync` section of the config. First the section-header comment block (lines 114-139):

input.old_string:
# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry is a full mirror (rsync
# --delete): everything under `path` is synced EXCEPT the paths matched by
# that folder's exclude list. There is no include mechanism — to narrow what
# is synced, use a deeper `path` or add more excludes.
#
# Each exclude is an rsync filter pattern, relative to the folder's transfer
# root (for /home the root is its contents, so `.ssh/id_*` matches every
# user's .ssh). Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom, first match wins. Syntax:
#   - No leading / floats: matches at any depth (`.cache/nvidia` matches
#     `.cache/nvidia` and `alice/.cache/nvidia`).
#   - Leading / anchors to the folder root (`/lost+found`).
#   - A pattern with no / matches the final path component only (`nvidia`);
#     one containing a / or ** matches the full path.
#   - Trailing / restricts to directories (`node_modules/`).
#   - `*` matches within a segment, `**` matches across /.
# Full syntax (rsync "FILTER RULES"): https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1
#
# Excluded below: machine-specific keys/config, GPU/shader caches, VS Code
# regenerable caches. Users can override by editing this file.
#
# Always excluded automatically (not listed here, cannot be overridden): pc-switcher's
# own runtime files — ~/.local/share/pc-switcher/ (state, lock, logs), the uv-tool
# install, and the ~/.local/bin/pc-switcher shim (ADR-016).

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or

        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine

        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
        # Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
        # (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would

        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon
