toolUseResult.oldString:
# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry is a full mirror (rsync
# --delete): everything under `path` is synced EXCEPT the paths matched by
# that folder's exclude list. There is no include mechanism — to narrow what
# is synced, use a deeper `path` or add more excludes.
#
# Each exclude is an rsync filter pattern, relative to the folder's transfer
# root (for /home the root is its contents, so `.ssh/id_*` matches every
# user's .ssh). Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom, first match wins. Syntax:
#   - No leading / floats: matches at any depth (`.cache/nvidia` matches
#     `.cache/nvidia` and `alice/.cache/nvidia`).
#   - Leading / anchors to the folder root (`/lost+found`).
#   - A pattern with no / matches the final path component only (`nvidia`);
#     one containing a / or ** matches the full path.
#   - Trailing / restricts to directories (`node_modules/`).
#   - `*` matches within a segment, `**` matches across /.
# Full syntax (rsync "FILTER RULES"): https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1
#
# Excluded below: machine-specific keys/config, GPU/shader caches, VS Code
# regenerable caches. Users can override by editing this file.
#
# Always excluded automatically (not listed here, cannot be overridden): pc-switcher's
# own runtime files — ~/.local/share/pc-switcher/ (state, lock, logs), the uv-tool
# install, and the ~/.local/bin/pc-switcher shim (ADR-016).

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      # Excludes are rsync filter patterns relative to /home/janfr. A leading /
      # anchors to the top level of the home dir. pc-switcher's own runtime files
      # (~/.local/share/pc-switcher, its uv-tool install, the ~/.local/bin shim)
      # are excluded automatically and need not be listed (ADR-016).
      excludes:

        # --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
        # SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
        - /.ssh/id_*
        # NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
        # you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
        # laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
        # other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

        # GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
        # would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
        - /.local/share/keyrings

        # NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
        # store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
        # pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

        # --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
        - /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
        - /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
        - /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
        - /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
        - /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
        - /.config/monitors.xml~
        - /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

        # --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
        - /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
        - /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
        - /.cache/thumbnails
        - /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
        - /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

        # --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
        # (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
        - /.cache/google-chrome
        - /.cache/spotify
        - /.cache/electron
        - /.cache/IBKR Desktop
        - /.cache/falco-app-updater
        - /.cache/plasmashell
        - /.config/Code/Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedData
        - /.config/Code/GPUCache
        - /.config/Code/Code Cache
        - /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
        - /.config/Claude/Cache
        - /.config/Claude/Code Cache
        - /.config/Claude/GPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
        - /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
        - /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

        # --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
        # Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
        # names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
        # from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
        # local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
        # so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
        # fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
        # Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
        # (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
        - /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
        - /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

        # --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
        # Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
        # content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
        # IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
        # are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
        - /.local/share/flatpak

        # --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
        - /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

        # --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
        # Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
        # Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
        # is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
        # conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
        # daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
        # links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
        # to those daemons entirely.
        # NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
        # Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
        # (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
        # leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
        # syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
        - /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
        - /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
        - /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
        - /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

        # --- Session runtime junk ---
        - /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

content.text:
Migration complete and verified — the config loads and passes schema validation.

What changed:

`~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml` — the `folder_sync` folder entry now uses `filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter` instead of the inline `excludes:` list. The section-header comment was updated to describe the new `filter_file` + `.pcswitcher-filter` model. (The old `excludes` field is no longer a valid key — the schema now rejects it — so this migration was required, not just cosmetic.)

`~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter` (new) — all your excludes ported verbatim to native rsync syntax (each `- pattern`), with every rationale comment preserved. Leading `/` anchors are kept as-is; they remain correct because your `path` is `/home/janfr`, so `/` anchors to the top of that home dir.

Two things to note:
- I added `- /.ssh/authorized_keys`, which your old config did not exclude. Mirroring it overwrites the target's copy and locks the sync out of its own target. It's now in the shipped default for this reason. Remove that line if you deliberately want it synced.
- I preserved your deliberate choices to keep `known_hosts`, `.gnupg`, `.password-store`, `.dropbox-dist`, and `.koofr-dist` **synced** (not excluded).

I did not add a `/root` entry — your config only had `/home/janfr`, so I left the folder set unchanged. Your cache strategy is also unchanged (exclude specific caches, keep dev caches). The new `+`/`-` first-match-wins syntax now also lets you flip to "drop all of `.cache` except a keep-list" if you ever prefer that; say the word if you want it restructured that way.

Your old inline list is still recoverable from git/backup, and `config.yaml.bak-stale-schema` was left untouched.

file.content:
# PC-switcher configuration file
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
# Documentation: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher
# Full config reference: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/blob/main/docs/configuration.md

# =============================================================================
# Logging Configuration (3-setting model)
# =============================================================================
# Controls where log messages appear based on their severity level.
#
# Log levels (from most to least verbose):
#   DEBUG (10)    - Internal diagnostics, very verbose
#   FULL (15)     - Operational details (file-level sync info)
#   INFO (20)     - High-level progress and status updates
#   WARNING (30)  - Unexpected but non-fatal conditions
#   ERROR (40)    - Recoverable errors
#   CRITICAL (50) - Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort

logging:
  # file: Floor level for log FILE output (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  #       Messages at or above this level are written to the log file.
  file: DEBUG

  # tui: Floor level for TERMINAL output during sync
  #      Messages at or above this level are shown in the terminal UI.
  tui: INFO

  # external: Additional floor for THIRD-PARTY libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
  #           External library logs must pass BOTH this floor AND the
  #           destination floor (file or tui) to appear in output.
  #           Use DEBUG to see SSH connection details, WARNING to suppress noise.
  external: WARNING

# =============================================================================
# Sync Jobs Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Enable or disable optional sync jobs
# Note: Only jobs explicitly listed here will be discovered and run
# Set to false to disable a job (e.g., dummy_fail: false)

sync_jobs:
  # Test jobs (for development/testing)
  dummy_success: true    # Test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false      # Test job that fails at configurable progress

  folder_sync: true          # Sync /home and /root directories (Phase 1)

  # Future jobs (uncomment when available):
  # packages: true        # Sync apt, snap, flatpak packages
  # docker: false         # Sync Docker images, containers, volumes
  # vms: false            # Sync KVM/virt-manager VMs
  # k3s: false            # Sync k3s cluster state

# =============================================================================
# Disk Space Monitoring
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for disk space checks before and during sync
# Threshold format: percentage (e.g., "20%") or absolute (e.g., "50GiB")

disk_space_monitor:
  # Minimum free space required before sync starts
  preflight_minimum: "20%"

  # Minimum free space required during sync (CRITICAL - abort if below)
  runtime_minimum: "15%"

  # Free space threshold for warnings during sync
  warning_threshold: "25%"

  # Seconds between disk space checks during sync
  check_interval: 30

# =============================================================================
# Btrfs Snapshot Management
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for automatic btrfs snapshots before and after sync
# IMPORTANT: Subvolumes must match YOUR system's btrfs layout

btrfs_snapshots:
  # List of btrfs subvolumes to snapshot
  # Check your system: sudo btrfs subvolume list /
  # Common layouts:
  #   - Ubuntu default: ["@", "@home"]
  #   - Single root: ["@"]
  subvolumes:
    - "@"
    - "@home"

  # Number of recent sync sessions to always retain snapshots for,
  # irrespective of age (max_age_days below)
  keep_recent: 3

  # Maximum age in days for snapshot retention (optional)
  # Note that always keeping 'keep_recent' snapshots takes precedence.
  # Uncomment to enable age-based cleanup:
  # max_age_days: 7

# =============================================================================
# Job-Specific Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Each sync job can have its own configuration section
# Configuration keys must match the job name

# Test job configurations (examples)
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on target machine

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail

# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry mirrors `path` (rsync --delete)
# minus the paths matched by its filter rules. Two authoring surfaces are
# available, both native rsync filter syntax (`+` include, `-` exclude,
# first-match-wins):
#   - filter_file: a per-folder central filter, spliced via rsync `merge`.
#     Optional; omit it for a folder that needs no central rule list (the
#     runtime excludes and any .pcswitcher-filter files still apply).
#   - .pcswitcher-filter: a tree-wide per-directory file, spliced via rsync
#     `dir-merge`; it inherits into subdirectories and can re-include a
#     subtree a broader exclude would otherwise drop (e.g. keep ~/.cache/uv
#     while dropping the rest of ~/.cache).
# GLOBAL-FIRST precedence: the central filter_file is evaluated before any
# .pcswitcher-filter, so central policy always wins. pc-switcher's own runtime
# files (ADR-016) are excluded first of all and can never be re-exposed by
# either surface.
#
# See docs/configuration.md for the full filter-rule syntax and a guide for
# users coming from .gitignore.

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter

    - path: /root
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/root.filter

# Future job configurations will be added here when features are implemented:

return self._conn

    async def connect(self) -> None:
        """Establish SSH connection to target.

        Respects ~/.ssh/config automatically via asyncssh.
        Connection will use keepalive to detect failures proactively.
        """
        self._conn = await asyncssh.connect(
            self._target,
            keepalive_interval=self._keepalive_interval,
            keepalive_count_max=self._keepalive_count_max,
        )
        # Publish connected event with initial latency (0 for now, or could measure)
        self._event_bus.publish(ConnectionEvent(status="connected", latency=0.0))

    async def disconnect(self) -> None:
        """Close the SSH connection gracefully."""
        if self._conn:
            self._conn.close()

# Python Development Conventions

Apply to Python code/projects. New or owner-controlled projects: use defaults. Existing: follow project config (Python version, deps, tests, lint, CI) unless explicitly migrating. Use modern standards for supported version.

## Python Version and Modern Practices

- New Python projects: target >=3.14
- Existing projects: use modern syntax for configured version
- Use modern type syntax: e.g. `str | None` instead of `Optional[str]`, `list[str]` instead of `List[str]`
- Include `from __future__ import annotations` when improves annotation behavior for Python version
- Use `@override` for method overrides when available in Python version
- Prefer `StrEnum` for string-based enums when available in Python version
- Prefer `Path` from pathlib for local filesystem paths

## Project Setup and Developer Workflows

- MUST use uv for running code, managing dependencies in new projects
- Existing projects: use project's workflow. Don't replace Poetry/Hatch/tox/Makefiles unless asked
- Use plain uv commands: `uv sync`, `uv run ...`, `uv add`, etc. Avoid `uv pip`. NEVER use `uv pip install` unless project intentionally uses uv's pip-compatible interface
- Use `codespell` for checking typos in code/comments
- Use `ruff` for linting, formatting
- Use `basedpyright` for type checking
- Use `pytest` for testing

## Code Quality Standards

- Full type annotations for function signatures, modern syntax per Python version
- Import types from correct modules (e.g., use `collections.abc` not `typing` for collection types)
- Proper error handling with specific exception types, meaningful messages
- Single responsibility principle, keep functions focused
- Use context managers for resource management
- Prefer composition over inheritance
- Self-documenting code with meaningful names
- Follow PEP 8 (max 119 chars), use Python strengths, avoid anti-patterns

## Documentation Philosophy

- Document architectural decisions, design patterns
- Concise docstrings: focus on "why" not obvious "what"
- Explain complex logic rationales inline when needed
- Use backticks for variable/parameter names in docstrings
- Avoid repetitive or obvious comments

## Testing Strategy

- Small focused tests, clear locality. Use repo's layout (colocated, `tests/` tree, doctests)
- Avoid trivial tests that test language itself
- Simple assertions over complex pytest fixtures when possible
- Test edge cases, errors, boundary values
- Mock external dependencies, I/O operations appropriately
- Mocked integration behavior: add real-dep integration coverage where practical
- Never use `assert False` - raise `AssertionError` with explanation instead

## Performance Optimization Techniques

- Use appropriate data structures (sets for membership, deque for queues, etc.)
- Implement generators for memory-efficient iteration over large datasets
- Apply caching strategies where beneficial
- Utilize built-in functions/libraries (itertools, collections, functools)
- Consider algorithmic complexity, choose efficient algorithms
- Profile code using cProfile, line_profiler, or memory_profiler before optimizing

## Error Handling and Type Checking

- Use `# pyright: ignore` sparingly, always with explanation
- Raise `AssertionError` with descriptive messages for invariant violations
- Handle exceptions at appropriate levels, avoid bare except
- Use specific exception types that convey meaning

## File Operations Best Practices

- Prefer `Path` objects for local filesystem paths
- Atomic file writing for user data, config, state or other where partial output harmful
- Use context managers for file operations
- Handle encoding explicitly (default UTF-8)

## Core Principles

- Pragmatic: avoid over-engineering, premature optimization
- Leverage stdlib first. Use third-party packages sparingly
- Code clarity over clever tricks
- Aim for functional, clean, maintainable, performant code

content.content:
# Python Development Commands

New or owner-controlled projects → uv. Existing repos → follow project's setup unless asked.

## Package Management with uv

Prefer uv (new projects). Skip `uv pip` unless project uses it. Don't replace other package managers without ask.

```bash
# Initialize new project
uv init

# Add dependencies
uv add package_name
uv add --dev ipykernel pytest ruff basedpyright  # dev dependencies

# Sync dependencies
uv sync

# Show installed packages
uv tree
uv tree --package package_name

# Run commands in virtual environment
uv run python script.py
uv run pytest
```

## Testing with pytest

```bash
# Run all tests
uv run pytest

# Run tests with coverage
uv run pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing

# Run specific test file or function
uv run pytest tests/test_module.py
uv run pytest tests/test_module.py::test_function

# Run tests matching pattern
uv run pytest -k "pattern"

# Run tests with verbose output
uv run pytest -v

# Run tests and stop on first failure
uv run pytest -x
```

## Linting and Formatting with ruff

```bash
# Format code
uv run ruff format .

# Check linting issues
uv run ruff check .

# Fix auto-fixable issues
uv run ruff check --fix .

# Check specific file
uv run ruff check path/to/file.py
```

## Type Checking with basedpyright

```bash
# Type check entire project
uv run basedpyright

# Type check specific file
uv run basedpyright path/to/file.py
```

## Code Quality Checks

```bash
# Run all quality checks (typical CI pipeline)
uv run ruff format --check .
uv run ruff check .
uv run basedpyright
uv run pytest

# Check for common typos
uv run codespell
```

## Profiling and Performance

```bash
# Profile with cProfile
uv run python -m cProfile -s cumtime script.py

# Profile with line_profiler
uv run kernprof -l -v script.py

# Memory profiling
uv run python -m memory_profiler script.py
```

## Common Development Patterns

```bash
# Check outdated packages
uv tree --outdated

# Clean pyc files and caches only when explicitly requested
find . -type d -name "__pycache__" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type d -name ".pytest_cache" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type d -name ".ruff_cache" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type f -name "*.pyc" -delete
```

=== ADR-013 auth section ===
1:# ADR-013: rsync-over-SSH as User-Data Transport (Root via sudo)
8:Use rsync-over-SSH as the user-data transport, running rsync as root on both ends via sudo to preserve cross-owner file metadata across /home and /root.
13:- Run rsync as root on the source: `sudo rsync ...`
14:- Run rsync as root on the target: pass `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` in the local rsync invocation, over the existing normal-user SSH connection
15:- Both endpoints require a passwordless sudoers entry scoped to the rsync binary path: `username ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/rsync`
20:- Preserve the SSH identity under sudo: pass `-E` to sudo or explicitly specify `-i <identity_file>` in the `-e ssh` argument, because root's SSH environment differs from the normal user's (RESEARCH Pitfall 1)
23:- Do NOT enable root SSH login; the SSH session remains as the normal user — privilege escalation applies only to the rsync binary via sudo
27:PC-switcher syncs /home and /root between machines. This requires reading and writing files owned by multiple users and preserving POSIX ACLs, xattrs, and owner/group metadata — all of which need root access on both ends.
35:- rsync runs as root on both ends: local `sudo rsync` on the source; `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` on the target, reached over the normal-user SSH connection (D-05)
36:- Root privilege is required to read/write files of all owners across /home and /root and to preserve owner/group metadata

=== ADR-013 auth section ===
1:# ADR-013: rsync-over-SSH as User-Data Transport (Root via sudo)
8:Use rsync-over-SSH as the user-data transport, running rsync as root on both ends via sudo to preserve cross-owner file metadata across /home and /root.
13:- Run rsync as root on the source: `sudo rsync ...`
14:- Run rsync as root on the target: pass `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` in the local rsync invocation, over the existing normal-user SSH connection
15:- Both endpoints require a passwordless sudoers entry scoped to the rsync binary path: `username ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/rsync`
20:- Preserve the SSH identity under sudo: pass `-E` to sudo or explicitly specify `-i <identity_file>` in the `-e ssh` argument, because root's SSH environment differs from the normal user's (RESEARCH Pitfall 1)
23:- Do NOT enable root SSH login; the SSH session remains as the normal user — privilege escalation applies only to the rsync binary via sudo
27:PC-switcher syncs /home and /root between machines. This requires reading and writing files owned by multiple users and preserving POSIX ACLs, xattrs, and owner/group metadata — all of which need root access on both ends.
35:- rsync runs as root on both ends: local `sudo rsync` on the source; `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` on the target, reached over the normal-user SSH connection (D-05)
36:- Root privilege is required to read/write files of all owners across /home and /root and to preserve owner/group metadata

Verified the auth model: pc-switcher connects to the target **as the normal login user** (root SSH login is not used — only the `rsync` binary is elevated via `sudo` on each end, per ADR-013), authenticating with the source user's SSH identity (agent key via `SSH_AUTH_SOCK`, or a default identity). So the target authenticates against **that user's** `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. Mirroring it locks the sync out only if the resulting file lacks the source machine's public key — which is exactly why your setup (every machine's key present) is safe.

# See docs/configuration.md "Filter rules" and "Coming from .gitignore".

# --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---

# target's copy (which does not list the source), so the target rejects the
# source's next connection and locks the sync out of its own target.
- /.ssh/authorized_keys
# NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
# you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
# laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
# other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

# GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
# would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.

# pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

# --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
- /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
- /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache

- /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
- /.cache/thumbnails
- /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
- /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

# --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
# (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
- /.cache/google-chrome
- /.cache/spotify
- /.cache/electron
- /.cache/IBKR Desktop

# --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---

# --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---

- /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

# --- Session runtime junk ---
- /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

file.content:
### Keep a subfolder inside an excluded one

To drop most of a directory but keep selected children — for example drop `~/.cache` but keep the dev-tool caches so offline work doesn't re-download them — re-include the children before excluding the rest:

```
+ .cache/
+ .cache/uv/***
+ .cache/pip/***
- .cache/*
```

The `/***` (not `/**`) matters here: it re-includes the `uv` and `pip` directory entries themselves, so the following `- .cache/*` can't drop them and rsync descends to copy their contents. The shipped `home.filter` already does this; add more `+ .cache/<tool>/***` lines for other caches you want to keep.

### Coming from .gitignore

The syntax resembles `.gitignore`, with three differences worth knowing:

- **Signs, not `!`** — `- pattern` excludes, `+ pattern` re-includes.
- **First match wins** (the opposite of gitignore) — order rules from specific to general.
- **Only a leading `/` anchors** — a middle slash does not, so for `/home` keep patterns unanchored to match under every user's home.

Otherwise it matches gitignore (basenames, a trailing `/` for directories, `*`/`**`/`[…]`), and a `.pcswitcher-filter` behaves like a committed `.gitignore`.

### Always excluded

pc-switcher's own files are always excluded and cannot be re-included, so a sync never disturbs the target's sync state or the running install: `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/`, `~/.local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher`, and `~/.local/bin/pc-switcher`.

### Coming from .gitignore
input.new_string:
The `/***` (not `/**`) matters here: it re-includes the `uv` and `pip` directory entries themselves, so the following `- .cache/*` can't drop them and rsync descends to copy their contents. The shipped `home.filter` already does this; add more `+ .cache/<tool>/***` lines for other caches you want to keep.

### `authorized_keys`

pc-switcher connects to the target **as the normal login user** (root SSH login is never used — only the `rsync` binary is elevated with `sudo` on each end; see ADR-013), authenticating with that user's SSH identity. The target therefore checks the connection against **that user's** `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`, which must contain the source machine's public key.

Because `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` is under a synced folder, a full mirror overwrites the target's copy with the source's. If the source's copy does not list the source machine's own public key, the mirror deletes the key the target was using to authorize the sync — and the **next** sync's SSH connection is rejected. The sync locks itself out of its own target.

Pick one of two approaches:

1. **Exclude it (safest default).** Each machine keeps its own independent access list. The shipped `home.filter` and `root.filter` do this:

   ```
   - .ssh/authorized_keys
   ```

2. **Sync it as one shared fleet access list.** Only safe if the file lists **every** pc-switcher machine's public key — including the machine's own — so a mirror can never drop the key the next sync needs. Since the file is identical everywhere once synced, it must be the union of all machines' keys.

   To set this up, before the first sync build that union in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on one machine, then let the sync propagate it. On each machine the key to add is the public half of the SSH identity pc-switcher connects with (the one your agent offers, e.g. `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`):

   ```bash
   # Collect each machine's public key (run per machine, copy the output)
   cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

   # On the machine you're seeding, append every machine's key (its own included),
   # deduplicate, and keep permissions tight:
   sort -u ~/.ssh/authorized_keys -o ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
   chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
   ```

   Then remove the `- .ssh/authorized_keys` line from the relevant filter file so it syncs.

Note that `/root` has its own `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` (`/root/.ssh/authorized_keys`) — but the SSH session is the normal user's, not root's, so only the **user's** `authorized_keys` gates the connection. Apply the same reasoning to `root.filter` only if you have a workflow that logs into the fleet as root directly.

### Coming from .gitignore

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Configuration Reference

Complete reference for `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`. Run `pc-switcher init` to write the annotated default file (`src/pcswitcher/default-config.yaml`), then edit it.

The config is validated against a JSON Schema on load (`src/pcswitcher/schemas/config-schema.yaml`). Unknown top-level keys and unknown job names are rejected. Both source and target run with the same config: it is copied to the target during sync (step 10 of the sync sequence).

## `logging`

Controls where log messages appear, based on severity. Three independent floors — a message is emitted to a destination when its level is at or above that destination's floor.

```yaml
logging:
  file: DEBUG        # floor for the log file (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  tui: INFO          # floor for terminal output during sync
  external: WARNING  # additional floor for third-party libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
```

### Log levels

| Level | Value | Description |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| DEBUG | 10 | Internal diagnostics, very verbose |
| FULL | 15 | Operational details (file-level sync info) |
| INFO | 20 | High-level progress (job start/complete) |
| WARNING | 30 | Unexpected but non-fatal conditions |
| ERROR | 40 | Recoverable errors |
| CRITICAL | 50 | Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort |

`file` and `tui` set the floor for their destination. `external` is an *additional* floor applied only to third-party library logs: an external log must clear **both** `external` and the destination floor (`file` or `tui`) to appear. Use `external: DEBUG` to surface asyncssh connection details; keep it at `WARNING` to suppress library noise.

Log files are written in JSON-lines format to `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/`.

Debug SSH connection issues:

```yaml
logging:
  file: DEBUG
  tui: INFO
  external: DEBUG  # show asyncssh debug logs
```

Quiet terminal, full log file:

```yaml
logging:
  file: DEBUG   # still record everything to the file
  tui: ERROR    # show only errors in the terminal
  external: ERROR
```

## `sync_jobs`

Enables or disables optional sync jobs. Only job names listed here are discovered and run; unknown names fail validation.

```yaml
sync_jobs:
  dummy_success: true   # test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false     # test job that fails at a configurable time
  folder_sync: true     # sync /home and /root via rsync
```

Future jobs (`packages`, `docker`, `vms`, `k3s`) are added here as they are implemented.

## `disk_space_monitor`

Disk-space checks before and during a sync. Thresholds are either a percentage (`"20%"`) or an absolute size (`"50GiB"`, `"500MiB"`, `"50GB"`, `"500MB"`).

```yaml
disk_space_monitor:
  preflight_minimum: "20%"   # required free space before sync starts
  runtime_minimum: "15%"     # required free space during sync — CRITICAL, aborts if crossed
  warning_threshold: "25%"   # logs a WARNING while free space is below this
  check_interval: 30         # seconds between checks during sync (5–300)
```

`preflight_minimum` is checked on both hosts before any mutation; a shortfall aborts the sync. `runtime_minimum` is enforced by a background monitor while jobs run and aborts the sync if crossed. `warning_threshold` only logs.

## `btrfs_snapshots`

Automatic btrfs snapshots taken before and after a sync (the rollback point). Subvolumes **must** match your machine's layout — check with `sudo btrfs subvolume list /`.

```yaml
btrfs_snapshots:
  subvolumes:        # subvolume names, each must start with @
    - "@"
    - "@home"
  keep_recent: 3     # always retain snapshots for the N most recent sync sessions
  # max_age_days: 7  # optional: also delete snapshots older than N days
```

`keep_recent` takes precedence over `max_age_days`: the N most recent sessions are always kept regardless of age. `max_age_days` is optional; omit it to disable age-based cleanup.

## `dummy_success` / `dummy_fail`

Per-job config for the test jobs (development only).

```yaml
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20   # seconds to run on source
  target_duration: 20   # seconds to run on target

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10
  target_duration: 10
  fail_at: 12           # elapsed seconds at which to fail
```

## `folder_sync`

Folders to sync via rsync-over-SSH, running as root on both ends to preserve ownership, permissions, ACLs, and xattrs.

```yaml
folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter
    - path: /root
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/root.filter
```

Each folder is mirrored to the target, minus the paths its filter rules exclude. Set `enabled: false` to skip a folder. A filtered-out file is left untouched on the target if it already exists there, so machine-specific files (SSH keys, Tailscale config) can stay independent on each machine.

### Filter rules

Filter rules decide what is and isn't synced. They live in two kinds of file:

- **`filter_file`** — the folder's main rule list. Optional: omit it for a folder that needs no central rule list, and only the runtime excludes and any `.pcswitcher-filter` files apply. `pc-switcher init` writes `home.filter` and `root.filter` next to `config.yaml`; edit them to taste. If a folder *does* configure a `filter_file` but the file is missing, the sync stops with an error.
- **`.pcswitcher-filter`** — a per-directory rule file that works like `.gitignore`: drop one into any directory and its rules apply to that directory and everything below. These files sync to the target too.

Each rule is `- pattern` to exclude or `+ pattern` to re-include. The **first** matching rule wins, so put a `+` re-include before the broader `-` it carves out of. If a path is matched in both a `filter_file` and a `.pcswitcher-filter`, the `filter_file` wins. This allows for central policy that can not be overridden by a per-directory file.

Pattern syntax:

- names match in full, never as substrings: each wildcard-free component must match a whole file or directory name — `foo` matches an entry named exactly `foo`, not `fool` or `afoo`. Use wildcards for partial names (`foo*`, `*foo*`).
- a pattern with no `/` is matched against the final path component (a single file or directory name); a pattern with a `/` is matched against the whole path below the folder root
- a leading `/` anchors to the folder root; without it a pattern matches at any depth
- a trailing `/` matches directories only; without it a pattern matches either a file or a directory of that name
- `?` matches one character, `*` matches within a path segment, `**` matches across `/`, and `[…]` is a character class
- `dir/**` matches a directory's contents but **not** the directory entry itself; `dir/***` matches the directory *and* everything in it. Reach for `/***` when a later exclude (like `- .cache/*`) would otherwise drop the directory and stop rsync from descending into it

| Pattern | Matches |
|---------|---------|
| `.ssh/id_*` | `id_*` inside any `.ssh` directory |
| `.config/tailscale` | a `tailscale` file or directory inside any `.config` |
| `/lost+found` | `lost+found` only at the folder root |
| `*.tmp` | any `.tmp` file, at any depth |
| `node_modules/` | any directory named `node_modules` |

Full pattern reference: rsync's manpage, "FILTER RULES" section — https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1

### Keep a subfolder inside an excluded one

To drop most of a directory but keep selected children — for example drop `~/.cache` but keep the dev-tool caches so offline work doesn't re-download them — re-include the children before excluding the rest:

```
+ .cache/
+ .cache/uv/***
+ .cache/pip/***
- .cache/*
```

The `/***` (not `/**`) matters here: it re-includes the `uv` and `pip` directory entries themselves, so the following `- .cache/*` can't drop them and rsync descends to copy their contents. The shipped `home.filter` already does this; add more `+ .cache/<tool>/***` lines for other caches you want to keep.

### Coming from .gitignore

The syntax resembles `.gitignore`, with three differences worth knowing:

- **Signs, not `!`** — `- pattern` excludes, `+ pattern` re-includes.
- **First match wins** (the opposite of gitignore) — order rules from specific to general.
- **Only a leading `/` anchors** — a middle slash does not, so for `/home` keep patterns unanchored to match under every user's home.

Otherwise it matches gitignore (basenames, a trailing `/` for directories, `*`/`**`/`[…]`), and a `.pcswitcher-filter` behaves like a committed `.gitignore`.

### Always excluded

pc-switcher's own files are always excluded and cannot be re-included, so a sync never disturbs the target's sync state or the running install: `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/`, `~/.local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher`, and `~/.local/bin/pc-switcher`.

lines: The `/***` (not `/**`) matters here: it re-includes the `uv` and `pip` directory entries themselves, so the following `- .cache/*` can't drop them and rsync descends to copy their contents. The shipped `home.filter` already does this; add more `+ .cache/<tool>/***` lines for other caches you want to keep.
+### `authorized_keys`

+pc-switcher connects to the target **as the normal login user** (root SSH login is never used — only the `rsync` binary is elevated with `sudo` on each end; see ADR-013), authenticating with that user's SSH identity. The target therefore checks the connection against **that user's** `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`, which must contain the source machine's public key.

+Because `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` is under a synced folder, a full mirror overwrites the target's copy with the source's. If the source's copy does not list the source machine's own public key, the mirror deletes the key the target was using to authorize the sync — and the **next** sync's SSH connection is rejected. The sync locks itself out of its own target.
+Pick one of two approaches:
+1. **Exclude it (safest default).** Each machine keeps its own independent access list. The shipped `home.filter` and `root.filter` do this:
+   - .ssh/authorized_keys

+2. **Sync it as one shared fleet access list.** Only safe if the file lists **every** pc-switcher machine's public key — including the machine's own — so a mirror can never drop the key the next sync needs. Since the file is identical everywhere once synced, it must be the union of all machines' keys.

+   To set this up, before the first sync build that union in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on one machine, then let the sync propagate it. On each machine the key to add is the public half of the SSH identity pc-switcher connects with (the one your agent offers, e.g. `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`):
+   ```bash
+   # Collect each machine's public key (run per machine, copy the output)
+   cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
+   # On the machine you're seeding, append every machine's key (its own included),
+   # deduplicate, and keep permissions tight:
+   sort -u ~/.ssh/authorized_keys -o ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
+   chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
+   Then remove the `- .ssh/authorized_keys` line from the relevant filter file so it syncs.

+Note that `/root` has its own `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` (`/root/.ssh/authorized_keys`) — but the SSH session is the normal user's, not root's, so only the **user's** `authorized_keys` gates the connection. Apply the same reasoning to `root.filter` only if you have a workflow that logs into the fleet as root directly.
### Coming from .gitignore
The syntax resembles `.gitignore`, with three differences worth knowing:

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# PC-switcher configuration file
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
# Documentation: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher
# Full config reference: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/blob/main/docs/configuration.md

# =============================================================================
# Logging Configuration (3-setting model)
# =============================================================================
# Controls where log messages appear based on their severity level.
#
# Log levels (from most to least verbose):
#   DEBUG (10)    - Internal diagnostics, very verbose
#   FULL (15)     - Operational details (file-level sync info)
#   INFO (20)     - High-level progress and status updates
#   WARNING (30)  - Unexpected but non-fatal conditions
#   ERROR (40)    - Recoverable errors
#   CRITICAL (50) - Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort

logging:
  # file: Floor level for log FILE output (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  #       Messages at or above this level are written to the log file.
  file: DEBUG

  # tui: Floor level for TERMINAL output during sync
  #      Messages at or above this level are shown in the terminal UI.
  tui: INFO

  # external: Additional floor for THIRD-PARTY libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
  #           External library logs must pass BOTH this floor AND the
  #           destination floor (file or tui) to appear in output.
  #           Use DEBUG to see SSH connection details, WARNING to suppress noise.
  external: WARNING

# =============================================================================
# Sync Jobs Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Enable or disable optional sync jobs
# Note: Only jobs explicitly listed here will be discovered and run
# Set to false to disable a job (e.g., dummy_fail: false)

sync_jobs:
  # Test jobs (for development/testing)
  dummy_success: true    # Test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false      # Test job that fails at configurable progress

  folder_sync: true          # Sync /home and /root directories (Phase 1)

  # Future jobs (uncomment when available):
  # packages: true        # Sync apt, snap, flatpak packages
  # docker: false         # Sync Docker images, containers, volumes
  # vms: false            # Sync KVM/virt-manager VMs
  # k3s: false            # Sync k3s cluster state

# =============================================================================
# Disk Space Monitoring
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for disk space checks before and during sync
# Threshold format: percentage (e.g., "20%") or absolute (e.g., "50GiB")

disk_space_monitor:
  # Minimum free space required before sync starts
  preflight_minimum: "20%"

  # Minimum free space required during sync (CRITICAL - abort if below)
  runtime_minimum: "15%"

  # Free space threshold for warnings during sync
  warning_threshold: "25%"

  # Seconds between disk space checks during sync
  check_interval: 30

# =============================================================================
# Btrfs Snapshot Management
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for automatic btrfs snapshots before and after sync
# IMPORTANT: Subvolumes must match YOUR system's btrfs layout

btrfs_snapshots:
  # List of btrfs subvolumes to snapshot
  # Check your system: sudo btrfs subvolume list /
  # Common layouts:
  #   - Ubuntu default: ["@", "@home"]
  #   - Single root: ["@"]
  subvolumes:
    - "@"
    - "@home"

  # Number of recent sync sessions to always retain snapshots for,
  # irrespective of age (max_age_days below)
  keep_recent: 3

  # Maximum age in days for snapshot retention (optional)
  # Note that always keeping 'keep_recent' snapshots takes precedence.
  # Uncomment to enable age-based cleanup:
  # max_age_days: 7

# =============================================================================
# Job-Specific Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Each sync job can have its own configuration section
# Configuration keys must match the job name

# Test job configurations (examples)
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on target machine

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail

# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry mirrors `path` (rsync --delete)
# minus the paths matched by its filter rules. Two authoring surfaces are
# available, both native rsync filter syntax (`+` include, `-` exclude,
# first-match-wins):
#   - filter_file: a per-folder central filter, spliced via rsync `merge`.
#     Optional; omit it for a folder that needs no central rule list (the
#     runtime excludes and any .pcswitcher-filter files still apply).
#   - .pcswitcher-filter: a tree-wide per-directory file, spliced via rsync
#     `dir-merge`; it inherits into subdirectories and can re-include a
#     subtree a broader exclude would otherwise drop (e.g. keep ~/.cache/uv
#     while dropping the rest of ~/.cache).
# GLOBAL-FIRST precedence: the central filter_file is evaluated before any
# .pcswitcher-filter, so central policy always wins. pc-switcher's own runtime
# files (ADR-016) are excluded first of all and can never be re-exposed by
# either surface.
#
# See docs/configuration.md for the full filter-rule syntax and a guide for
# users coming from .gitignore.

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter

    - path: /root
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/root.filter

# Future job configurations will be added here when features are implemented:

content.text:
All green. Done.

Changes made:

Your live system (`~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter`)
- Removed the `- /.ssh/authorized_keys` exclude — `authorized_keys` now syncs, per your setup. Replaced with a comment stating it's an intentional shared fleet access list and must contain every machine's key (its own included), or a sync locks itself out.

Repo docs (the ask)
- `docs/configuration.md` — new `### authorized_keys` subsection under `folder_sync`: explains the auth model (connects as the normal user, target checks that user's `authorized_keys`), why a mirror can lock the sync out, and the two options — **exclude it** (safest default, with the rule) or **sync it as a shared fleet list** with concrete `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` / `sort -u` / `chmod 600` steps and the note that `/root` has its own file but the SSH session is the normal user's.
- `src/pcswitcher/default-config.yaml` — added an `authorized_keys` note to the `folder_sync` comment block covering the same choice, pointing to the doc.

Consistency (not asked, but the shipped defaults contradicted the docs otherwise)
- `src/pcswitcher/home.filter` / `root.filter` — reworded the `authorized_keys` exclude comments from a one-sided "locks you out" warning to "excluded by default; to sync it, make it a shared fleet list and drop this line," pointing to the doc. The exclude rule itself is unchanged, so the safe default stands.

Verified: filter/CLI unit tests (16) pass; the auth model claims are from `connection.py`, `jobs/folder_sync.py`, and ADR-013, not memory.

Note: the repo changes are uncommitted on `main`. Want me to branch and commit them?

attachment.content:
# Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
# Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
# is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
# conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
# daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
# links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
# to those daemons entirely.

content.content:
1	# pc-switcher central filter file for the /home/janfr folder_sync entry.
2	# Native rsync filter syntax: `+` includes, `-` excludes, first-match-wins.
3	# The transfer root is /home/janfr's contents, so a leading `/` anchors to the
4	# top level of this home dir (e.g. `- /.ssh/id_*` == /home/janfr/.ssh/id_*).
5	# See docs/configuration.md "Filter rules" and "Coming from .gitignore".
6	
7	# --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
8	# SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
9	- /.ssh/id_*
10	# NOTE: .ssh/authorized_keys IS synced here (intentionally not excluded). This is
11	# a shared fleet access list: it lists EVERY pc-switcher machine's public key,
12	# including this machine's own, so a mirror never drops the key the next sync's
13	# SSH connection depends on. Keep every machine's key in it or a sync will lock
14	# itself out of its own target. See docs/configuration.md "authorized_keys".
15	# NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
16	# you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
17	# laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
18	# other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.
19	
20	# GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
21	# would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
22	- /.local/share/keyrings
23	
24	# NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
25	# store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
26	# pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.
27	
28	# --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
29	- /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
30	- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
31	- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
32	- /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
33	- /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
34	- /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
35	- /.config/monitors.xml~
36	- /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays
37	
38	# --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
39	- /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
40	- /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
41	- /.cache/thumbnails
42	- /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
43	- /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches
44	
45	# --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
46	# (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
47	- /.cache/google-chrome
48	- /.cache/spotify
49	- /.cache/electron
50	- /.cache/IBKR Desktop
51	- /.cache/falco-app-updater
52	- /.cache/plasmashell
53	- /.config/Code/Cache
54	- /.config/Code/CachedData
55	- /.config/Code/GPUCache
56	- /.config/Code/Code Cache
57	- /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
58	- /.config/Claude/Cache
59	- /.config/Claude/Code Cache
60	- /.config/Claude/GPUCache
61	- /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
62	- /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
63	- /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)
64	
65	# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
66	# Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
67	# names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
68	# from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
69	# local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
70	# so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
71	# fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
72	# Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
73	# (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
74	- /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
75	- /.local/share/Trash            # trash can
76	
77	# --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
78	# Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
79	# content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
80	# IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
81	# are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
82	- /.local/share/flatpak
83	
84	# --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
85	- /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link
86	
87	# --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
88	# Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
89	# Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
90	# is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
91	# conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
92	# daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
93	# links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
94	# to those daemons entirely.
95	# NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
96	# Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
97	# (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
98	# leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
99	# syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
100	- /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
101	- /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
102	- /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
103	- /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon
104	
105	# --- Session runtime junk ---
106	- /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot
107

# pc-switcher central filter file for the /home/janfr folder_sync entry.
# Native rsync filter syntax: `+` includes, `-` excludes, first-match-wins.
# The transfer root is /home/janfr's contents, so a leading `/` anchors to the
# top level of this home dir (e.g. `- /.ssh/id_*` == /home/janfr/.ssh/id_*).
# See docs/configuration.md "Filter rules" and "Coming from .gitignore".

# --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
# SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
- /.ssh/id_*
# NOTE: .ssh/authorized_keys IS synced here (intentionally not excluded). This is
# a shared fleet access list: it lists EVERY pc-switcher machine's public key,
# including this machine's own, so a mirror never drops the key the next sync's
# SSH connection depends on. Keep every machine's key in it or a sync will lock
# itself out of its own target. See docs/configuration.md "authorized_keys".
# NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
# you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
# laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
# other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

# GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
# would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
- /.local/share/keyrings

# NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
# store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
# pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

# --- Machine/hardware-specific: wrong or useless on different hardware ---
- /.cache/nvidia                 # GPU shader cache — tied to this GPU model
- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache
- /.cache/mesa_shader_cache_db
- /.nv                           # NVIDIA compute cache
- /.cache/fontconfig             # font-path cache — rebuilt per machine
- /.config/monitors.xml          # GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors
- /.config/monitors.xml~
- /.local/share/kscreen          # KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays

# --- Desktop indexes & thumbnails: machine-local DBs, rebuilt automatically ---
- /.local/share/baloo            # KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target
- /.cache/tracker3               # GNOME file index
- /.cache/thumbnails
- /.cache/gnome-software         # software-center metadata cache
- /.cache/*.kcache               # KDE Plasma theme/icon caches

# --- App runtime caches: churn constantly, no offline-work value ---
# (Dev tooling caches like .cache/uv, .cache/pip, .npm are deliberately NOT here.)
- /.cache/google-chrome
- /.cache/spotify
- /.cache/electron
- /.cache/IBKR Desktop
- /.cache/falco-app-updater
- /.cache/plasmashell
- /.config/Code/Cache
- /.config/Code/CachedData
- /.config/Code/GPUCache
- /.config/Code/Code Cache
- /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
- /.config/Claude/Cache
- /.config/Claude/Code Cache
- /.config/Claude/GPUCache
- /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
- /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
- /.var/app/*/cache              # flatpak per-app caches (Firefox ~1.3G, etc.)

# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
# Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
# names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
# from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
# local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
# so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
# fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
# Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
# (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
- /.config/Claude/vm_bundles
- /.local/share/Trash            # trash can

# --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
# Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
# content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
# IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
# are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
- /.local/share/flatpak

# --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
- /.var/app/org.signal.Signal    # Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link

# --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
# Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
# Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
# is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
# conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
# daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
# links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
# to those daemons entirely.
# NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
# Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
# (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
# leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
# syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
- /.dropbox                      # daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine
- /Dropbox                       # content — handled by the Dropbox daemon
- /.koofr                        # daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync)
- /Koofr                         # content — handled by the Koofr daemon

# --- Session runtime junk ---
- /.xsession-errors              # X session log for this boot

aiTitle: Update config.yaml for folder sync filters

content.text:
Verified against your actual cache — one repo env (43M), and it contains a **Python venv**, not a download cache. Two things it hardcodes:

- shebangs are absolute: `#!/home/janfr/.cache/pre-commit/repos0c54eis/py_env-python3.14/bin/python`
- its base interpreter is a specific uv-managed build: `/home/janfr/.local/share/uv/python/cpython-3.14.3-linux-x86_64-gnu/bin/python3.14` (from `pyvenv.cfg`)

Answering your two questions:

Stale? No — not in the "serves wrong results" sense. pre-commit keys each env by repo + rev + declared dependencies and runs a health check before reusing one. A mismatched or broken env is detected and reinstalled, never silently used. The only thing that accumulates is dead envs for old revs (garbage, reclaimed by `pre-commit gc`) — space, not correctness.

Useful? Marginally, and less than uv/pip. uv and pip are relocatable content-addressed *download* caches — syncing them genuinely avoids re-downloading (uv especially, at 6.7G). pre-commit stores *built venvs* that hardcode absolute interpreter paths. It only pays off on the target if the target has the same `/home/janfr` home *and* that exact uv-managed CPython build. If not, the health check fails and pre-commit rebuilds it anyway — the synced copy was wasted, not harmful. Worst case is neutral; best case saves one ~1-minute hook reinstall.

My recommendation: **skip pre-commit**; keep `uv` + `pip` (the relocatable download caches that actually save network). ms-playwright (browser binaries, version-keyed) and node-gyp (header cache) are also download-style caches if you want the big offline wins, but they're optional.

Want me to go with `uv` + `pip` only, or add ms-playwright/node-gyp?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'

content.text:
Inspected and verified:

What it is: Playwright's browser download cache. Contents are version-pinned browser builds — `chromium-1228`, `chromium_headless_shell-1228`, `ffmpeg-1011` (646M). `playwright install` fetches these over the network. Unlike pre-commit, these are plain binaries keyed by build number, not venvs with hardcoded interpreter paths — so they're relocatable across your same-arch Linux machines. (There's also a separate `ms-playwright-go`, 253M, holding the Go port's browsers for versions 1.50.1 + 1.57.0, last touched May 30.)

Used? Yes — Playwright is a real dependency in active projects: `~/dev/Kapernikov/planner/e2e` (TS e2e suite) and `~/dev/scout` (Python, `scrape_web.py` + `pyproject.toml`). (I can't prove recent *runs* — the access time is just my own scan touching it — but it's declared in those projects' deps, so it's live, not abandoned.)

Useful to sync? Yes, more like uv/pip than pre-commit. It's a version-keyed download cache, relocatable, backing e2e tests you actually have set up. Syncing it saves a ~646M `playwright install` on the target and lets you run those suites offline. Downside is only space + lingering old builds when a project bumps Playwright (garbage, cleared by `playwright uninstall --all` or just deleting old build dirs).

`ms-playwright-go` (253M): same idea but I found no clear consumer (scout is Python, not Go), and it's older. I'd skip it unless you know what uses it.

So: `ms-playwright` is worth keeping; `node-gyp` (native-build header cache) and `ms-playwright-go` are marginal.

Proposed keep-list: `uv`, `pip`, `ms-playwright`. Want me to write that, or adjust?

content.text:
Verified against your installed Playwright (1.61.0) CLI — the full command list is: `open, codegen, install, uninstall, install-deps, cr/ff/wk, screenshot, pdf, show-trace, trace`.

There is **no selective GC** — no `uv cache prune` analog that drops only stale/unreferenced builds. What exists:

- `playwright uninstall` — removes the browsers *this* Playwright version uses.
- `playwright uninstall --all` — removes *all* Playwright browsers from every installation (nukes the whole `~/.cache/ms-playwright`).

So the closest equivalent is `uninstall --all` ≈ `uv cache clean` (purge everything), then reinstall. There's nothing like `uv cache prune` that keeps the cache bounded by removing only what's no longer needed.

Consequence for syncing: uv's cache is self-bounding (`prune`); ms-playwright is not. Old `chromium-<build>` dirs accumulate as projects bump Playwright — each ~150–300M — and nothing auto-reclaims them. The `.links/` dir tracks which installs reference the cache but doesn't trigger collection. Growth is slow (version bumps are infrequent), and if it bloats you reset with `uninstall --all` + reinstall.

Net: still fine to sync — just know it can drift upward over time and needs an occasional manual `uninstall --all`, unlike uv which you can `cache prune` to keep trim.

So keep-list options stand: `uv` + `pip` (self-bounding), optionally `+ ms-playwright` (useful, unbounded). Which do you want written?

content.text:
Verified from your cache:

What it is: node-gyp is the build tool npm uses to compile **native Node addons** — packages with C/C++ code (e.g. `better-sqlite3`, `bcrypt`). To compile against a given Node version it needs that version's C++ headers and libs, which it downloads once into `~/.cache/node-gyp/<version>/`. Your cache holds four Node versions (22.21.0 → 22.23.1), each ~63M of `include/` headers — 251M total.

Useful to sync? Low — the weakest of the candidates:

- It's only *used* when you build a native addon from source. Many npm packages ship prebuilt binaries (via `prebuild`/`node-pre-gyp`) and never touch node-gyp, so the cache may sit idle.
- It's just headers — re-downloaded trivially and quickly on the next native build (~63M, small vs uv/playwright).
- Like ms-playwright, no GC command: old Node-version dirs pile up (you already have 4); you'd manually delete stale ones.

So it saves little and drifts upward. I'd skip it.

Recap of where we landed on `.cache`:

- `uv` — keep (6.7G, self-bounding via `cache prune`, big win)
- `pip` — keep (relocatable download cache)
- `ms-playwright` — keep (646M, real offline win for e2e; unbounded, occasional manual `uninstall --all`)
- `pre-commit` — skip (built venvs, not reliably relocatable, self-heals)
- `node-gyp`, `ms-playwright-go` — skip (marginal / no clear consumer)

Proposed keep-list: `uv`, `pip`, `ms-playwright`. Shall I write it?

content.text:
Verified from your bundled node-gyp (12.1.0). Full command set: `build, clean, configure, rebuild, install, list, remove`.

No auto-GC/prune, but unlike Playwright it has **targeted removal**:

- `node-gyp list` — prints cached versions (confirmed it shows your four: 22.21.0, 22.22.1, 22.22.2, 22.23.1).
- `node-gyp remove <version>` — deletes that version's headers from `~/.cache/node-gyp`.

So you reclaim manually:

```bash
node-gyp list                 # see what's cached
node-gyp remove 22.21.0       # drop each stale one
node-gyp remove 22.22.1
node-gyp remove 22.22.2       # keep only 22.23.1 (current)
```

There's no single "prune all but the in-use version" command — you `list` then `remove` each. (Note: `node-gyp clean` is unrelated — it wipes the *current project's* `build/` output, not the header cache.)

So on self-bounding-ness, ranked: uv (`cache prune`, automatic-ish) > node-gyp (`remove <version>`, manual per-version) > ms-playwright (`uninstall --all` only, all-or-nothing).

Doesn't change my recommendation — node-gyp is still low-value to sync (headers, cheaply re-fetched, only used for native builds). Keep-list stands at `uv`, `pip`, `ms-playwright`. Want me to write it?

content.text:
Big finding — this is more significant than anything under `.cache`:

`~/.npm` is **6.9G** and it's **not under `~/.cache`**, so your current `home.filter` doesn't touch it — it's being synced wholesale right now. Breakdown:

- `_cacache` **4.2G** — npm's content-addressed package cache (downloaded tarballs + metadata). Same category as uv/pip: relocatable download cache.
- `_npx` **2.8G** — packages npx installed on-the-fly to run one-off commands. Throwaway.
- `_prebuilds` 1.1M

Also outside `.cache`: `~/.bun` **859M**.

Let me confirm npm's own GC commands (read-only):

content.text:
Yes — verified. npm/npx have their own caches and their own GC:

npm cache (`~/.npm`, 6.9G):

- `npm cache verify` — GC: garbage-collects unneeded data and rebuilds the index (the prune analog). Runs on `_cacache`.
- `npm cache clean --force` — full purge of `_cacache`.
- `npm cache npx ls` / `npm cache npx rm [<key>…]` — dedicated management for the `_npx` cache (list / remove entries).

So both parts are GC-able. Categories:

- `_cacache` (4.2G) — content-addressed download cache, relocatable, `npm cache verify` keeps it bounded. Exactly the same category as uv/pip.
- `_npx` (2.8G) — throwaway packages from one-off `npx <tool>` runs. No reuse value across machines; `npm cache npx rm` clears it.

bun (`~/.bun`, 859M): mixed, not all cache —

- `bin/` (98M) — the bun **binary** itself (not a cache).
- `install/` (762M) — bun's install/download cache + global packages (`install/cache` is droppable, same category as the others).

The bigger point: your `.cache` allowlist only governs `~/.cache`. These multi-GB caches live **elsewhere in `$HOME`** and are currently synced in full because nothing excludes them. So the real wins are:

- `~/.npm/_npx` (2.8G) — exclude, pure throwaway.
- `~/.npm/_cacache` (4.2G) and `~/.bun/install/cache` (762M) — keep only if you want offline npm/bun installs (consistent with keeping uv/pip); otherwise exclude, they re-fetch.

Two ways to proceed — your call:

1. Keep going tool-by-tool and I fold each verdict into `home.filter`.
2. Set a principle instead: "sync relocatable *download* caches that have a real offline-install win (uv, pip, npm `_cacache`, bun cache, ms-playwright); drop everything else regenerable (all of `.cache/*` not allowlisted, `_npx`, pre-commit, node-gyp)." I apply it across both `~/.cache` and the home-level caches in one pass.

Which do you want?

content.text:
Both confirmed — and you're right, I was wrong to call `_npx` throwaway.

`_npx` — it's a reuse cache, and your MCP servers live in it

Verified contents: only 7 entries (fairly clean, not junk-bloated), and they include `@upstash/context7-mcp` and `@modelcontextprotocol/*` — i.e. the MCP servers launched via `npx`.

How reuse works:
- Re-running the same `npx <spec>` reuses its `_npx/<hash>` entry — **no redownload**. (For an unpinned/`@latest` spec, npx may still do a quick network *metadata* check for a newer version, but it won't re-fetch the tarball if the resolved version is already cached; a pinned spec reuses fully offline.)
- Nuance: `_npx` entries are materialized *from* `_cacache`. If you drop `_npx` but keep `_cacache`, the next `npx` call rebuilds the entry from `_cacache` **offline** (a fast relink, no network). Only if *both* are gone does it re-download.

So for MCP servers: keeping `_npx` = instant startup; keeping just `_cacache` = quick offline rebuild on first launch. Either way, syncing at least `_cacache` means MCP servers never re-download on the target. `_npx` is genuinely keep-worthy. GC is selective: `npm cache npx ls` / `npm cache npx rm <key>` (no auto-prune).

Revised verdict: **keep `_npx`** (or at minimum `_cacache`).

bun — yes, GC exists

Verified: `bun pm cache` prints the cache path; `bun pm cache rm` clears it. Full-clear only — no selective prune (like Playwright's `uninstall --all`). The cache is `~/.bun/install/cache`.

Layout: `~/.bun/bin` (98M) is the bun **binary** Claude Code uses; `~/.bun/install` (762M) is the reuse/download cache + global packages. Keeping `bin/` means bun is present on the target without a separate install; the cache is a download cache like the others.

Where this leaves us: almost every big cache we've examined turns out to be a **relocatable reuse/download cache with real offline value** — uv, pip, npm `_cacache`, npm `_npx` (MCP servers), bun, ms-playwright. The genuine drops are the machine-local regenerables: GPU/shader caches, thumbnails, desktop indexes, browser *app* caches, pre-commit venvs, node-gyp headers.

That's exactly why the **principle** approach now looks better than tool-by-tool. Proposed principle:

- Keep (allowlist): relocatable download/reuse caches with an offline-install/startup win — under `.cache`: `uv`, `pip`, `ms-playwright`; outside `.cache` (leave synced): `~/.npm`, `~/.bun`.
- Drop: machine-local regenerables — everything else in `.cache/*`, plus keep excluding the GPU/shader/index/thumbnail/app-cache items already listed.

Want me to apply that (write the `.cache` allowlist for uv/pip/ms-playwright and leave `~/.npm`/`~/.bun` synced), or keep probing?
